column5:
Subacute subdural hematoma

Keyword findings:
subacute right SDH
almost exactly the same attenuation as underlying gray matter
displaced gray-white matter interface toward midline

Reasoning: The source-supported recognition move is isodense right convexity collection detected by interface shift. In the allowed sources, subacute SDH can be isodense to cortex on CT or iso- to hyperintense on T1/FLAIR MR, and diagnosis depends on crescentic extraaxial location plus mass effect, displaced cortical interface/CSF, or membrane behavior rather than conspicuous acute hyperdensity.

Subdural effusion
Subdural empyema
column21:
🧠 Subacute Subdural Hematoma
Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. Core Radiology was retrieved from the uploaded Core Radiology PDF, Neuro Brain, Cerebral trauma / Extra-axial hemorrhage / Subdural hematoma, book page label Neuro Brain: 701; RadPrimer and STATdx supplied the fused article/image package.

🔑 Summary
  • Subacute subdural hematoma is a crescentic extraaxial subdural collection in the approximately 3-day to 3-week window.
  • Core frames an isodense subdural hematoma as isoattenuating to gray matter in the subacute phase, approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
  • RadPrimer/STATdx define subacute SDH as about 3 days to 3 weeks old, with partially liquefied clot and resorbing blood products surrounded by granulation tissue membrane.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • NECT pattern is crescentic, iso- to hypodense, extraaxial, and may spread diffusely over a hemisphere.
  • When the collection is isodense to cortex, search for displaced gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inward displacement of CSF dots or cortical veins, increased mass effect, and white matter buckling.
  • SDH can cross cranial sutures because it is beneath the dura; Core also notes extension along the falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli.
  • Density evolves from acute hyperdensity toward isodensity around 7-10 days and hypodensity around 10-14 days; STATdx/RadPrimer state SDHs decrease by about 1.5 HU/day.
  • Mixed density or septations/internal membranes suggest different ages or recent hemorrhage into a preexisting collection.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal is iso- to hyperintense; T2 is often hyperintense; FLAIR does not suppress like CSF.
  • T1 C+ may show enhancing membranes; outer membrane enhancement supports late or unstable sSDH.
  • DWI signal varies with hematoma age; blood signal is not easily predictable, but a hyperintense outer rim can correlate with unstable membranes and future growth.
  • STATdx recommends FLAIR, DWI, and SWI/GRE in MR protocols for SDH/trauma evaluation.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Other subdural collections include effusion, hygroma, and empyema.
  • Pachymeningopathies can mimic dural thickening; in absent trauma history, look for intracranial hypotension signs.
  • Chronic dural sinus thrombosis remains a differential consideration for subdural-type collections and pachymeningeal findings.
🧬 Clinical / Path / Mechanism
  • Mechanism centers on tearing/stretching of bridging cerebral veins, especially in older adults with atrophic involutional changes and enlarged CSF spaces.
  • The outer membrane is tied to enlargement and repetitive hemorrhage; the inner membrane is tied to liquefaction.
  • Subtle delayed presentations include headache, seizure, or focal neurologic deficit.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Balanced bilateral isodense SDHs can hide because there is no obvious side-to-side density asymmetry.
  • In patients without trauma history, evaluate coagulation status and search for intracranial hypotension: brain sagging, dura-arachnoid thickening, engorged dural venous sinuses, and plump pituitary.
  • FLAIR is helpful for identifying small SDH/non-CSF collections but is inaccurate for staging hematoma chronicity.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Subacute SDH is the board trap where blood becomes gray-matter density: do not look only for bright blood; look for mass effect, buckled gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inwardly displaced CSF/veins, and membrane behavior.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. Core Radiology was retrieved from the uploaded Core Radiology PDF, Neuro Brain, Cerebral trauma / Extra-axial hemorrhage / Subdural hematoma, book page label Neuro Brain: 701; RadPrimer and STATdx supplied the fused article/image package.

🔑 Summary
  • Subacute subdural hematoma is a crescentic extraaxial subdural collection in the approximately 3-day to 3-week window.
  • Core frames an isodense subdural hematoma as isoattenuating to gray matter in the subacute phase, approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
  • RadPrimer/STATdx define subacute SDH as about 3 days to 3 weeks old, with partially liquefied clot and resorbing blood products surrounded by granulation tissue membrane.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • NECT pattern is crescentic, iso- to hypodense, extraaxial, and may spread diffusely over a hemisphere.
  • When the collection is isodense to cortex, search for displaced gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inward displacement of CSF dots or cortical veins, increased mass effect, and white matter buckling.
  • SDH can cross cranial sutures because it is beneath the dura; Core also notes extension along the falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli.
  • Density evolves from acute hyperdensity toward isodensity around 7-10 days and hypodensity around 10-14 days; STATdx/RadPrimer state SDHs decrease by about 1.5 HU/day.
  • Mixed density or septations/internal membranes suggest different ages or recent hemorrhage into a preexisting collection.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal is iso- to hyperintense; T2 is often hyperintense; FLAIR does not suppress like CSF.
  • T1 C+ may show enhancing membranes; outer membrane enhancement supports late or unstable sSDH.
  • DWI signal varies with hematoma age; blood signal is not easily predictable, but a hyperintense outer rim can correlate with unstable membranes and future growth.
  • STATdx recommends FLAIR, DWI, and SWI/GRE in MR protocols for SDH/trauma evaluation.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Other subdural collections include effusion, hygroma, and empyema.
  • Pachymeningopathies can mimic dural thickening; in absent trauma history, look for intracranial hypotension signs.
  • Chronic dural sinus thrombosis remains a differential consideration for subdural-type collections and pachymeningeal findings.
🧬 Clinical / Path / Mechanism
  • Mechanism centers on tearing/stretching of bridging cerebral veins, especially in older adults with atrophic involutional changes and enlarged CSF spaces.
  • The outer membrane is tied to enlargement and repetitive hemorrhage; the inner membrane is tied to liquefaction.
  • Subtle delayed presentations include headache, seizure, or focal neurologic deficit.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Balanced bilateral isodense SDHs can hide because there is no obvious side-to-side density asymmetry.
  • In patients without trauma history, evaluate coagulation status and search for intracranial hypotension: brain sagging, dura-arachnoid thickening, engorged dural venous sinuses, and plump pituitary.
  • FLAIR is helpful for identifying small SDH/non-CSF collections but is inaccurate for staging hematoma chronicity.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Subacute SDH is the board trap where blood becomes gray-matter density: do not look only for bright blood; look for mass effect, buckled gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inwardly displaced CSF/veins, and membrane behavior.

column5:
Subacute subdural hematoma

Keyword findings:
outer membrane thick and enhances uniformly
sSDH slightly hyperintense compared to sulcal CSF

Reasoning: The source-supported recognition move is unique RadPrimer axial membrane reinforcement. In the allowed sources, subacute SDH can be isodense to cortex on CT or iso- to hyperintense on T1/FLAIR MR, and diagnosis depends on crescentic extraaxial location plus mass effect, displaced cortical interface/CSF, or membrane behavior rather than conspicuous acute hyperdensity.

Differential:
Subdural hygroma
Subdural effusion
Subdural empyema

column7: NECT head. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill focuses on an isodense crescentic extraaxial collection where the decisive clue is mass effect despite cortex-like density.
Subacute subdural hematoma -> isodense crescentic subdural collection with inward displacement of CSF dots/gray-white interface and compressed sulci.
Subdural hygroma -> CSF-like collection without blood-product density or membrane behavior; use source-supported FLAIR/T1/DWI behavior if MR is available.
Subdural empyema -> source notes DWI may help exclude empyema; restricted purulent collection is the key mimic to separate when diffusion is obtained.

column7: NECT head. Based on the images, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Subacute subdural hematoma
Intracranial hypotension
Chronic dural sinus thrombosis
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill focuses on bilateral nearly isodense subdural collections when trauma history is absent, so the discriminator is the associated low-pressure/pachymeningeal search pattern.
Subacute subdural hematoma -> bilateral isodense crescentic collections with displaced cortical veins/interface.
Intracranial hypotension -> source-supported etiology clue is suprasellar cistern effacement, midbrain slumping, and plump pituitary in the same patient.
Chronic dural sinus thrombosis -> remains in the source differential for subdural-type collections and dural/pachymeningeal findings.

column7: MR brain. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill focuses on MR fluid characterization rather than CT density; the key source-supported clue is that subacute collections do not suppress on FLAIR like CSF.
Subacute subdural hematoma -> FLAIR non-suppression and T1 iso- to hyperintensity relative to CSF/cortex depending on age.
Subdural hygroma -> CSF-like collection; expected to behave more like CSF when sequence behavior is demonstrated.
Subdural empyema -> source-supported safety check is DWI, which may help exclude empyema.

column7: NECT head. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Subacute subdural hematoma
Chronic subdural hematoma
Acute-on-chronic subdural hemorrhage
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill focuses on mixed-density/septated subdural blood, where the discriminator is internal membranes plus recent hemorrhage into a preexisting collection.
Subacute subdural hematoma -> mixed-density subdural collection with source-described linear high attenuation suggesting internal membranes.
Chronic subdural hematoma -> more chronic-appearing low-density/nonacute collection when no recent high-attenuation component is emphasized.
Acute-on-chronic subdural hemorrhage -> recent hemorrhage into a preexisting collection is the key source-supported concept.

column7: DWI brain. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Subacute subdural hematoma
Subdural empyema
Unstable subdural membrane
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill focuses on DWI behavior, where the source warns that blood signal is not easily predictable and a hyperintense outer rim may indicate unstable membranes.
Subacute subdural hematoma -> mixed DWI signal with a hypointense rim; blood signal can be unpredictable.
Subdural empyema -> DWI is source-supported as helpful to exclude empyema in the membrane-enhancement MR cluster.
Unstable subdural membrane -> hyperintense outer rim on DWI correlates with unstable membranes and future SDH growth.

column16:
Source definition: A subdural hematoma in the subacute time window; RadPrimer/STATdx specify about 3 days to 3 weeks old, and Core describes an isodense subdural as occurring in the subacute phase approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
Refined operational definition based on the allowed source set: a crescentic extraaxial subdural blood collection beneath the dura, often partially liquefied/resorbing and potentially membrane-lined in the subacute interval.
Boundary: It is a blood-containing subdural hematoma, not a simple CSF hygroma/effusion or empyema.
Synonyms: sSDH.

  • In patients without trauma history, evaluate coagulation status and search for intracranial hypotension: brain sagging, dura-arachnoid thickening, engorged dural venous sinuses, and plump pituitary.
  • FLAIR is helpful for identifying small SDH/non-CSF collections but is inaccurate for staging hematoma chronicity.
⭐ Super Summary

column11: Why are older adults predisposed to subdural hematoma after minor trauma?
column12: Core and RadPrimer/STATdx tie SDH formation to tearing/stretching of cerebral bridging veins. In older adults, atrophic involutional changes enlarge the CSF spaces, so cerebral veins are stretched as they traverse the subdural space. Minor trauma can therefore tear these veins and allow blood to collect beneath the dura as a crescentic subdural hematoma. Imaging pivot: the collection is extraaxial and subdural, can spread across sutures, and may create mass effect even when its density becomes difficult to see.

🧠 Subacute Subdural Hematoma
Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. Core Radiology was retrieved from the uploaded Core Radiology PDF, Neuro Brain, Cerebral trauma / Extra-axial hemorrhage / Subdural hematoma, book page label Neuro Brain: 701; RadPrimer and STATdx supplied the fused article/image package.

🔑 Summary
  • Subacute subdural hematoma is a crescentic extraaxial subdural collection in the approximately 3-day to 3-week window.
  • Core frames an isodense subdural hematoma as isoattenuating to gray matter in the subacute phase, approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
  • RadPrimer/STATdx define subacute SDH as about 3 days to 3 weeks old, with partially liquefied clot and resorbing blood products surrounded by granulation tissue membrane.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • NECT pattern is crescentic, iso- to hypodense, extraaxial, and may spread diffusely over a hemisphere.
  • When the collection is isodense to cortex, search for displaced gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inward displacement of CSF dots or cortical veins, increased mass effect, and white matter buckling.

  • Mixed density or septations/internal membranes suggest different ages or recent hemorrhage into a preexisting collection.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal is iso- to hyperintense; T2 is often hyperintense; FLAIR does not suppress like CSF.
  • T1 C+ may show enhancing membranes; outer membrane enhancement supports late or unstable sSDH.

column11: Why can a subacute subdural hematoma become nearly invisible on noncontrast CT?
column12: RadPrimer/STATdx state that SDHs decrease by about 1.5 HU/day, become isodense with cortex by 7-10 days, and become hypodense by about 10-14 days; Core similarly states that an isodense SDH is isoattenuating to gray matter in the subacute phase. As clot liquefies and blood products resorb, attenuation approaches cortex rather than remaining acutely hyperdense. Imaging pivot: the diagnosis shifts from detecting bright blood to detecting indirect mass-effect signs: increased mass effect, white matter/gray-white interface buckling, compressed sulci, displaced CSF dots, and apparently thickened cortex.

column11: Why does outer membrane enhancement matter in late or unstable subacute SDH?
column12: RadPrimer/STATdx describe the subacute collection as partially liquefied clot/resorbing blood surrounded by granulation tissue membrane, with repetitive hemorrhage from the outer membrane causing enlargement. The outer membrane is thicker than the inner membrane in the contrast-enhanced examples and may enhance on T1 C+ imaging. Imaging pivot: enhancing outer membrane marks the biologically active membrane component of a late/unstable collection and helps separate subacute/chronic SDH membrane behavior from simple CSF-like subdural collections or pachymeningeal mimics.

column11: Why should bilateral subacute SDHs without trauma trigger a search for intracranial hypotension?
column12: RadPrimer/STATdx explicitly instruct that when sSDH appears without trauma history, the reader should evaluate coagulation status and search for intracranial hypotension signs. In the supplied same-patient CT cluster, bilateral isodense SDHs accompany effacement of the suprasellar cistern, midbrain slumping, and a plump pituitary. Imaging pivot: the subdural collections are not just trauma products; the associated low-pressure brain-sag pattern redirects the search toward intracranial hypotension as the etiology.

column13: What is the trap when a noncontrast CT shows no obvious hyperdense extraaxial blood, and what safety check prevents the miss?
column14:
Trap: Mistaking an isodense subacute SDH for normal cortex or mild cortical thickening because it is isoattenuating to gray matter.
Safety check: Actively inspect for increased mass effect, white matter/gray-white interface buckling, compressed sulci, and inwardly displaced CSF dots/cortical veins.
Key discriminator: Mass effect and displaced interfaces reveal the crescentic subdural collection even when attenuation matches cortex.
Why it happens: Subacute blood becomes isodense to cortex, so density contrast disappears before the mass-effect clues disappear.

column13: What is the trap with balanced bilateral isodense subdural collections, and what safety check prevents the miss?
column14:
Trap: Calling the scan normal because there is no obvious unilateral asymmetry or bright blood.
Safety check: Compare both gray-white interfaces and sulci for symmetric inward displacement/compression, and look for displaced CSF dots or cortical veins.
Key discriminator: Balanced bilateral subdural collections can compress both hemispheres symmetrically, hiding the usual side-to-side clue.
Why it happens: The collections are nearly isodense to cortex and bilateral, so both density and asymmetry cues are blunted.

column15: What is the source-supported age window for subacute SDH?
column16:
RadPrimer/STATdx: about 3 days to 3 weeks old.
Core: isodense SDH occurs in the subacute phase approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.

🧠 Subacute Subdural Hematoma

🔑 Summary

  • Core frames an isodense subdural hematoma as isoattenuating to gray matter in the subacute phase, approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
  • RadPrimer/STATdx define subacute SDH as about 3 days to 3 weeks old, with partially liquefied clot and resorbing blood products surrounded by granulation tissue membrane.

CT
  • NECT pattern is crescentic, iso- to hypodense, extraaxial, and may spread diffusely over a hemisphere.
  • When the collection is isodense to cortex, search for displaced gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inward displacement of CSF dots or cortical veins, increased mass effect, and white matter buckling.

  • T1 signal is iso- to hyperintense; T2 is often hyperintense; FLAIR does not suppress like CSF.

column15: For subacute SDH on NECT, what indirect clues reveal an isodense collection?
column16:
Increased mass effect, white matter/gray-white interface buckling, apparently thickened cortex, compressed sulci, and inwardly displaced dots of CSF/cortical veins.
Exam pivot: Look for distortion, not just hyperdensity.

column15: How does SDH density evolve on CT according to RadPrimer/STATdx?
column16:
SDHs decrease by ~ 1.5 HU/day.
By 7-10 days: blood becomes isodense with cortex.
By ~ 10-14 days: hypodense compared with adjacent brain.

column15: For SDH, what is the key suture/dural-attachment rule?
column16:
Because SDH lies beneath the dura, it can cross cranial sutures.
It does not cross dural attachments; source also notes extension along falx and tentorium surfaces.
Exam pivot: separates SDH from arterial epidural hematoma, which is limited by sutures.

Blood signal characteristics are not easily predictable on DWI.
Susceptibility is often present, but significance is uncertain.

🔑 Summary
  • Subacute subdural hematoma is a crescentic extraaxial subdural collection in the approximately 3-day to 3-week window.
  • Core frames an isodense subdural hematoma as isoattenuating to gray matter in the subacute phase, approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
  • RadPrimer/STATdx define subacute SDH as about 3 days to 3 weeks old, with partially liquefied clot and resorbing blood products surrounded by granulation tissue membrane.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • NECT pattern is crescentic, iso- to hypodense, extraaxial, and may spread diffusely over a hemisphere.
  • When the collection is isodense to cortex, search for displaced gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inward displacement of CSF dots or cortical veins, increased mass effect, and white matter buckling.
  • SDH can cross cranial sutures because it is beneath the dura; Core also notes extension along the falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli.
  • Density evolves from acute hyperdensity toward isodensity around 7-10 days and hypodensity around 10-14 days; STATdx/RadPrimer state SDHs decrease by about 1.5 HU/day.
  • Mixed density or septations/internal membranes suggest different ages or recent hemorrhage into a preexisting collection.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal is iso- to hyperintense; T2 is often hyperintense; FLAIR does not suppress like CSF.
  • T1 C+ may show enhancing membranes; outer membrane enhancement supports late or unstable sSDH.
  • DWI signal varies with hematoma age; blood signal is not easily predictable, but a hyperintense outer rim can correlate with unstable membranes and future growth.
  • STATdx recommends FLAIR, DWI, and SWI/GRE in MR protocols for SDH/trauma evaluation.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Other subdural collections include effusion, hygroma, and empyema.
  • Pachymeningopathies can mimic dural thickening; in absent trauma history, look for intracranial hypotension signs.
  • Chronic dural sinus thrombosis remains a differential consideration for subdural-type collections and pachymeningeal findings.
🧬 Clinical / Path / Mechanism
  • Mechanism centers on tearing/stretching of bridging cerebral veins, especially in older adults with atrophic involutional changes and enlarged CSF spaces.
  • The outer membrane is tied to enlargement and repetitive hemorrhage; the inner membrane is tied to liquefaction.
  • Subtle delayed presentations include headache, seizure, or focal neurologic deficit.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Balanced bilateral isodense SDHs can hide because there is no obvious side-to-side density asymmetry.
  • In patients without trauma history, evaluate coagulation status and search for intracranial hypotension: brain sagging, dura-arachnoid thickening, engorged dural venous sinuses, and plump pituitary.
  • FLAIR is helpful for identifying small SDH/non-CSF collections but is inaccurate for staging hematoma chronicity.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Subacute SDH is the board trap where blood becomes gray-matter density: do not look only for bright blood; look for mass effect, buckled gray-white interface, compressed sulci, inwardly displaced CSF/veins, and membrane behavior.

column15: What should be reported for subacute SDH on imaging?
column16:
Report mass effect, sulcal compression, midline shift/herniation if present, bilateral balance, mixed density suggesting rebleeding, and membrane enhancement.
Exam pivot: these features drive diagnostic confidence and communicate instability/mass effect.

column15: Which FLAIR chronicity caveat is explicitly stated for subdural hematomas?
column16:
FLAIR is inaccurate for staging chronicity of hematomas.
Exam pivot: FLAIR helps identify non-CSF subdural collections, but should not be used alone to age the hematoma.

column15: What CT appearance card should you know for subacute SDH?
column16:
NECT: crescentic, iso- to hypodense, extraaxial, may spread diffusely over a hemisphere.
Key clue when isodense: displaced gray-white interface/compressed sulci rather than hyperdense blood.
Exam pivot: diagnosis is often indirect in the subacute window.
